content.full_text: August 30, 1990 Fair Political Practices Commission Attn: John Wallace Counsel Legal Division P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 GROSS ACREAGE FOR THE CITY OF CARLSBAD COMMUNITY FACILITIES DISTRICT The attached schedule shows the gross acreage to be included in the first phase of the City of Carlsbad Citywide Community Facilities District. The schedule has been broken down into five categories including residential, commercial, industrial and office, agricultural and other uses. The other uses category generally includes land in the cities non-residential reserve zoning, open space, school property, utility property, and a few other unusual classifications of land which would otherwise be undevelopable. You will note that most of the City’s industrially zoned property is found in Zone 5 with smaller amounts being found in Zone 15 and 22. The attached map of the Bridge and Thoroughfare District should help explain why the taxes in area I and II differ. Property in Area I would have been conditional to pay for interchanges on I-5. Area II has no such obligation. I hope this information is useful. If you need additional information, please feel free to call. JAMES F. ELLIOTT JFE:tlg Enclosures The table provided shows the total gross acreage by zone for the City of Carlsbad, City Wide Community Facilities District, broken down into residential, commercial, industrial and office, agricultural, and other uses. The zones included in the first phase of CFD are listed, along with their respective acres and percentages for each category. Here is a summary of the table: | Zone | Residential Acres | Residential Percent | Commercial Acres | Commercial Percent | Industrial and Office Acres | Industrial and Office Percent | Agricultural Acres | Agricultural Percent | Other Uses Acres | Other Uses Percent | Total All Types Acres | Total All Types Percent |
content.full_text_markdown:
August 30, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
Attn: John Wallace
Counsel Legal Division
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

GROSS ACREAGE FOR THE CITY OF CARLSBAD COMMUNITY FACILITIES DISTRICT

The attached schedule shows the gross acreage to be included in the first phase of the City of Carlsbad Citywide Community Facilities District. The schedule has been broken down into five categories including residential, commercial, industrial and office, agricultural and other uses. The other uses category generally includes land in the cities non-residential reserve zoning, open space, school property, utility property, and a few other unusual classifications of land which would otherwise be undevelopable. You will note that most of the City’s industrially zoned property is found in Zone 5 with smaller amounts being found in Zone 15 and 22.

The attached map of the Bridge and Thoroughfare District should help explain why the taxes in area I and II differ. Property in Area I would have been conditional to pay for interchanges on I-5. Area II has no such obligation.

I hope this information is useful. If you need additional information, please feel free to call.

JAMES F. ELLIOTT

JFE:tlg

Enclosures

Residential: 4,633.69 acres (100%)
Commercial: 271.19 acres (5.87%)
Industrial and Office: 1,065.92 acres (23.00%)
Agricultural: 1,106.94 acres (24.00%)
Other Uses: 3,183.35 acres (68.50%)

(1) Other uses include Non-residential reserves, open space, school and utility property and other unusual classifications (Zones OS,NRR,N,U,E,JH,H)
(2) Industrial / Office includes zones - O,PI

The image is a map of the Bridge & Thoroughfare District, with shaded and unshaded areas indicating different tax rate zones for community facilities districts. The map includes street names such as Marron Rd, Elm Ave, Cannon Rd, Poinsettia Ln, and others.

Handwritten notes on the map state:
- "Community Facilities District Tax Rates for Area II Apply in the Unshaded Area."
- "Community Facilities District Tax Rates for Area I Apply for All Property in the Shaded Area."

Below the map, there is a legend for district boundaries and a table listing tax rates per unit:
- Single Family: $530/Unit
- Condominium: $424/Unit
- Apartments: $318/Unit

At the bottom, it states: "For fees on commercial and industrial uses, please contact the City of Carlsbad Development Processing Section at 438-1161."

**CITY OF CARLSBAD**
1200 CARLSBAD VILLAGE DRIVE
CARLSBAD, CALIFORNIA 92008-1989
(619) 434-2891

VINCENT F. BIONDO, JR.

RONALD R. BALL
ASSISTANT CITY ATTORNEY

March 21, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

RE: REQUEST FOR FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION OPINION
COUNCIL MEMBER MAMAUX'S PARTICIPATION IN DECISIONS REGARDING ESTABLISHMENT OF A CITY-WIDE COMMUNITY FACILITIES DISTRICT

The City of Carlsbad as one means of implementing its award winning growth management program contemplates the formation of a $117,000,000 city-wide community facilities district under the provisions of the Mello-Roos Community Facilities Act of 1982. The bond issue proceeds will be used for public facilities including a library, city hall, regional park, major arterial roads and freeway interchanges. The program does not relieve developers of any responsibility but allows the City to "finance the developer fees" and construct the public facilities required by growth management. Mr. Mamaux's employer owns approximately three percent of the land which would be subject to the levy of a special tax if the district is established.

Enclosed is a copy of a "Request for Opinion of the Fair Political Practices Commission" prepared by the City's bond counsel giving the facts which we ask the Commission to consider in reaching its opinion. Also enclosed is a copy of our growth management ordinance and detailed information on the proposed district.

Please let me know if you need any further information in order to expedite your response to this request.

Very truly yours,

VINCENT F. BIONDO, JR.
City Attorney

rmh
enclosures

c: Mayor and Council
    City Manager
    Warren Diven, Bond Counsel

REQUEST FOR OPINION OF THE
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

QUESTION

Based upon the facts set forth below, does Councilmember John Mamaux have a financial interest which would preclude him under the provisions of Government Code § 87100 from participating in the decision by the City Council of the City of Carlsbad to form a citywide community facilities district under the provisions of the Mello-Roos Community Facilities Act of 1982?

In 1986 the City Council of the City of Carlsbad adopted Chapter 21.90 of the City’s Municipal Code establishing a growth management plan for the City. A copy of Chapter 21.90 is attached for your reference. Chapter 21.90 required the preparation of a Citywide Facilities and Improvements Plan which was adopted by the City Council in 1986 ("Citywide Plan"). The Citywide plan established numerous geographical zones within the City and required the establishment of plans for each zone (collectively, the "Local Plans"). The Citywide Plan and Local Plans create standards for the financing and construction of a wide variety of public facilities including parks, roads, freeway intersections, libraries, fire stations, civic center and other public facilities. These Plans have been adopted by the City Council. The Plans identify establishment of a community facilities district as one method for financing the public facilities and services identified in the various Plans.

Section 21.90.130 provides that no development permit shall be approved unless such a permit is consistent with the Local Facilities Management Plan and unless provisions for all facilities and improvements related to the development project are provided or funded in a manner acceptable to the City Council.

In order to guarantee the provision and funding of certain facilities which are required prior to the issuance of development permits within the City, the formation of a community facilities district has been proposed. The community facilities district, if formed, would authorize the issuance of approximately $117,000,000 in special tax bonds, which would be secured by the levy of special taxes against undeveloped property within the City of Carlsbad. The bonds would be utilized to finance all or a portion of a library, City hall facility, regional park, local roads, freeway interchanges, and a utility maintenance warehouse, as well as incidental expenditures and costs of issuance for the bonds. The City has previously determined that the construction or guarantee of the financing of the construction of these facilities is a necessary prerequisite under Chapter 21.90 and the Citywide Plan and various Local Plans to the issuance of development permits.

As mentioned above, the special tax would be levied at least initially only against currently undeveloped property. For property designated for residential development, an annual special tax would be levied for each developable acre until the issuance of a building permit for that property. At the time of issuance of a building permit, such residential property would be subject to a one time special development tax. Following payment of the special development tax, the residential property would not be subject to the levy of further special taxes for this community facilities district, although the property might be subject to facilities fees established pursuant to Section 21.90.050 of Chapter 21.90.

Prior to development, property designated for commercial and industrial development would pay an annual special tax per developable acre. Upon the issuance of a building permit for the development of such property, the owners of such commercial or industrial property would have the option of paying a one time development tax or assuming an annual special development tax based upon the square footage of the building area. A copy of the proposed special tax formula is attached hereto.

In November, 1989, Councilmember Mamaux became employed by Center Mortgage, a lending institution, the principal office of which is located in the City of Carlsbad. The primary focus of the lending activities of Center Mortgage is to provide financing for $300,000 to $700,000 commercial and industrial developments. Center Mortgage is owned by C&N Bank Corp., a California corporation the sole shareholder which is Dean Greenberg. Mr. Greenberg is the owner of Center Development, a land development company located in the City of Carlsbad which is the owner of 69 parcels which include approximately 129.66 net taxable acres designated for commercial or industrial use within Local Facilities Management Zone 5 and 1 parcel which includes approximately 95.8 net taxable acres within Local Facilities Management Zone 25 of the City. Zone 5 contains a total of 207 taxable parcels which include 910 taxable acres owned by 40 different property owners. Zone 25 contains a total of 5 taxable parcels which include 191.52 taxable acres owned by 5 different property owners. The property owned by Center Development would be subject to the levy of special taxes if the community facilities district is established.

The Center Development property will also receive the same benefit received by other undeveloped properties throughout the City from the establishment of the community facilities district, the levy of special taxes and the issuance of bonds, ie., the satisfaction of the condition precedent to the issuance of development permits by the establishment of a method for guaranteeing the financing of the construction of public facilities as required by Chapter 21.90. The total number of taxable acres within the City, which may be subject to the levy of special taxes if the community facilities district is established, is 7393 acres which are located in Zones 4 through 25, inclusive. The total number of parcels throughout these zones is 1,451 and these parcels are owned by 496 owners. Zones 1 through 3 are not proposed to be included in the community facilities district because these zones have guaranteed the provision and funding of the facilities required for these zones through other methods.
